participants.reasoning: The extracted participants match the official inclusion and exclusion criteria and recruitment source, with only minor wording differences that do not change the meaning.
participants.extracted: Eligible participants were African American men and women with angiographic evidence of at least one coronary artery with 50% or greater stenosis. Exclusion criteria included acute myocardial infarction, stroke, or coronary revascularization within the previous 3 months; chronic heart failure with an ejection fraction below 20%; cognitive impairment; and any noncardiac life-threatening illness. Participants continued usual medical care throughout the study. They were identified from the African American Heart Health Registry of the Medical College of Wisconsin and other Milwaukee-area hospital databases, and each patient’s physician had to give permission for study participation.
participants.official:
Recruited from the African American Heart Health Registry of the Medical College of Wisconsin and other databases of Milwaukee area hospitals. The study ran in 2 phases due to funding difficulties: phase 1 from March 1998 to April 2003 and phase 2 from March 2004 to July 2007. All phase 1 subjects were invited to participate in phase 2. Subjects provided written informed consent separately for each of the 2 phases.

interventions.reasoning: The extracted description matches the official interventions on the TM course structure, practice schedule, follow-up meetings, and the health education control’s matched format and heart-healthy home practice.
interventions.extracted: Participants were randomly assigned to either a Transcendental Meditation (TM) program or a cardiovascular health education (HE) control program, while continuing usual medical care. The TM intervention consisted of a standardized 7-step course taught by a certified instructor, including six 1.5–2 hour individual and group sessions, followed by regular follow-up and maintenance meetings (weekly for the first month, biweekly for the next two months, then monthly thereafter). Subjects were instructed to practice the TM technique for 20 minutes twice daily while sitting comfortably with eyes closed. The HE control was designed to match the TM arm for instructional time, instructor attention, participant expectancy, and social support, using standard health education materials; participants were advised to spend at least 20 minutes per day practicing heart-healthy behaviors such as exercise, healthy meal preparation, and nonspecific relaxation.
interventions.official:
Intervention(duration and setting not reported):
The TM technique is described as a simple, natural, effortless procedure that is practised 20 minutes twice a day while sitting comfortably with eyes closed (Roth 1994). Standard teaching materials and format were used. The TM technique was taught in a 7‐step course of instruction comprising six 1.5‐ to 2‐hour individual and group meetings taught by an instructor certified by Maharishi Foundation USA. outcomes.reasoning: The extracted outcomes match the official primary and secondary clinical endpoints and include the listed intermediate measures, with only additional detail rather than contradiction.
outcomes.extracted: The trial planned to measure a primary clinical endpoint of time to first event for the composite of all-cause mortality, nonfatal myocardial infarction, or nonfatal stroke, assessed during follow-up over a maximum of 9.3 years (mean 5.4 ± 2.4 years). A secondary clinical endpoint was time to first event for the composite of cardiovascular mortality, nonfatal myocardial infarction, nonfatal stroke, coronary revascularization, or hospitalization for ischemic heart disease (non-MI) or heart failure, assessed during the same follow-up period. Additional secondary intermediate outcomes included blood pressure, smoking, alcohol use, body mass index, diet, exercise, and psychological distress. Blood pressure was measured as three seated mercury sphygmomanometer readings at baseline, month 3, and every 6 months thereafter. BMI was calculated as weight/height² at the same visits. Diet was assessed with the Block Dietary Food Consumption Questionnaire, smoking and alcohol use with weekly recall questionnaires, and exercise with a modified Minnesota Leisure Time Physical Activity Questionnaire; these lifestyle measures were collected at baseline and then annually for intermediate outcome assessment. Psychological distress was assessed annually using the Center for Epidemiological Studies Depression Scale (CES-D) for depression, the Cook-Medley Hostility Inventory composite score for hostility, and the Anger Expression scale for anger-in, anger-out, anger-control, and total anger.
outcomes.official: Follow‐up at 5.4‐year average follow‐up: composite of all‐cause mortality, nonfatal MI, or nonfatal stroke (primary outcome), composite of cardiovascular mortality, nonfatal MI, nonfatal stroke, coronary revascularisation, or hospitalisation for IHD (non‐MI) or heart failure (secondary outcome), SBP, DBP, depression, BMI, smoking
